prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
French: Le silence entre nous disait plus que n'importe quel argument.
Note: The French sentence is a direct translation with a slight reordering of the words. The phrase "n'importe quel" means "any", and "argument" is left untranslated.

Japanese: 私たちの間の沈黙は、どんな議論でも言い表せないほど多くを語っていた。
Note: The Japanese sentence uses the word "pinkmondo" (pinkmon-do) to mean "silence" and "argumento" (a-rue-men-to) to mean "argument". The phrase "do-nin no" means "between", and "hoka ni nirai" means "more than".

Arabic: немات بيننا قالت أكثر من أي مناقشة.
Note: The Arabic sentence uses the word "nemat" (نمات) to mean "silence" and "min al-munaza" (من المنازلة) to mean "argument". The phrase "bayna" (بين) means "between", and "akthar min" (أكثر من) means "more than".
